title: Restore VA Accountability Act of 2025
stage_label: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.
plain_summary: This bill makes it easier and faster for the Veterans Affairs Department to fire, demote, or suspend their supervisors, managers, and other employees who do poorly at their jobs or break rules. The VA only needs strong evidence instead of going through long, complicated processes to take action against these workers. Employees who face punishment can still get a lawyer and appeal the decision, but they have less time and fewer chances to fight it. The bill protects workers who report problems or illegal activities from being punished unfairly. These new rules also apply to cases that started back in 2017.
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Restore Department of Veterans Affairs Accountability Act of 2025 or the Restore VA Accountability Act of 2025.

sections.title: Section 2 Supervisors: Removal, Demotion, or Suspension Based on Performance or Misconduct
sections.text:
This section adds new rules for disciplining supervisors at the Veterans Affairs Department. The Secretary of Veterans Affairs can fire, demote, or suspend a supervisor if there is substantial evidence that their performance or misconduct is serious enough to warrant such action.

When deciding whether to take disciplinary action, the decision maker must only consider two factors. First, they must look at how serious the offense was and how it relates to the person's job duties and responsibilities. They must consider if the offense was done on purpose or by accident, if it was done for personal gain or with bad intentions, and if it happened repeatedly. Second, they must consider the person's job level and type of work, including whether they supervise others or handle money, and how important their position is.

The Secretary must review the initial decision and approve it if there is substantial evidence to support it. The supervisor being disciplined has certain rights. They must be given advance notice of the action and a file with all the evidence. They can have a lawyer or other representative help them. They can file a complaint through an internal process that the Secretary will set up with help from the Assistant Secretary for Accountability and Whistleblower Protection.

The Secretary's final decision that is not appealed and any decision from the internal complaint process cannot be changed. The normal federal employee protection procedures do not apply to these cases. The Secretary can take disciplinary action without first putting the supervisor on a performance improvement plan.

There are strict time limits for these procedures. The entire process from notice to final decision cannot take more than fifteen business days. The supervisor has seven business days to respond to the notice. The Secretary's final decision must be made within fifteen business days of giving notice and must be in writing with specific reasons. The internal complaint process must take fewer than twenty one days after the final decision.

Supervisors who are harmed by a final decision can ask a federal court to review it. However, they cannot appeal to the Merit Systems Protection Board or other administrative judges. When a court reviews the case, it can only overturn the Veterans Affairs Department's action if it was arbitrary, done without following proper procedures, or not supported by substantial evidence. Courts cannot reduce the punishment unless there is a constitutional issue.

If a supervisor is demoted, they will be moved to a lower grade position that they are qualified for and that reduces their pay. Starting on the day of demotion, they will receive the lower pay rate. They cannot be put on paid administrative leave while appealing. They only get paid if they report to work or use approved vacation, sick, family medical, military, or court leave. If they do not report to work or get approval to use leave, they will not receive pay or benefits.

There are special protections for whistleblowers. If a supervisor is seeking help from the Office of Special Counsel for a prohibited personnel practice, the Secretary cannot discipline them without approval from the Special Counsel. If a supervisor made a whistleblower disclosure to the Assistant Secretary for Accountability and Whistleblower Protection, the Secretary must wait until that matter is fully resolved before taking disciplinary action.

The Office of Special Counsel can end an investigation of a prohibited personnel practice involving a Veterans Affairs Department employee. They must give the employee a written explanation for ending the investigation. This explanation cannot be used as evidence in court or administrative proceedings unless the employee agrees.

These rules apply to any performance or misconduct that happened on or after the date when the Department of Veterans Affairs Accountability and Whistleblower Protection Act of 2017 became law.

The section defines several important terms. A covered individual is a Veterans Affairs Department employee who is a supervisor or management official, including various types of appointees, but not senior executives, certain medical appointees, people still in their trial period, or political appointees. Misconduct includes neglecting duties, wrongdoing, or refusing a directed job reassignment. A political appointee is someone in high level executive positions, certain Senior Executive Service positions, or confidential policy making positions. Suspend means putting an employee in a temporary status without duties and pay for more than fourteen days. Whistleblower disclosure has the meaning given in another section of the law.

sections.title: Section 3 Senior Executives: Modification of Procedures to Remove, Demote, or Suspend Based on Performance or Misconduct
sections.text:
This section changes the rules for disciplining senior executives at the Veterans Affairs Department. The Secretary must now have substantial evidence before taking disciplinary action against a senior executive.

When making an initial decision about whether to reprimand, suspend, reassign, demote, or remove a senior executive, the decision maker must only consider two factors. First, they must look at how serious the offense was and how it relates to the person's job duties and responsibilities, including whether it was done on purpose or by accident, for personal gain or with bad intentions, and if it happened repeatedly. Second, they must consider the person's job level and type of work, including supervisory responsibilities and how important their position is.

The Secretary must review the initial decision and approve it if there is substantial evidence to support it. The internal complaint process must be completed within twenty one days after the final decision. Courts cannot reduce the punishment imposed on the senior executive unless there is a constitutional issue.

These rules apply to any misconduct or performance issues of senior executives that happened on or after the date when the Department of Veterans Affairs Accountability and Whistleblower Protection Act of 2017 became law.

sections.title: Section 4 Modification of Disciplinary Procedures for Employees of the Department of Veterans Affairs
sections.text:
This section changes the disciplinary rules for regular Veterans Affairs Department employees. The Secretary must now have substantial evidence before taking disciplinary action.

When making an initial decision about whether to remove, demote, or suspend an employee, the decision maker must only consider five factors. First, the nature and seriousness of the offense and how it relates to their job duties, including whether it was intentional or accidental, done for gain or with bad intentions, and if it happened repeatedly. Second, the employee's job level and type of work, including supervisory responsibilities. Third, the employee's past disciplinary record. Fourth, the employee's past work record, including length of service, job performance, ability to work with others, and dependability. Fifth, any special circumstances around the offense such as unusual job stress, personality problems, mental health issues, harassment, or bad faith by others involved.

The Secretary must review the initial decision and approve it if there is substantial evidence to support it. The Secretary can take disciplinary action without first putting the employee on a performance improvement plan.

When employees appeal disciplinary actions, administrative judges and the Merit Systems Protection Board cannot reduce the punishment unless there is a constitutional issue. Federal courts also cannot reduce the punishment unless there is a constitutional issue.

These procedures override any union contract that conflicts with them. The rules apply to any performance or misconduct that happened on or after the date when the Department of Veterans Affairs Accountability and Whistleblower Protection Act of 2017 became law.

The definition of covered individual is expanded to include various types of appointees and hybrid employees, but supervisors and management officials are now excluded from the regular employee disciplinary process.

For Veterans Health Administration employees, the Secretary can choose to handle disciplinary matters for supervisors and management officials under the new supervisor rules, the regular employee rules, or regular federal employment law.
